sections.heading: What is the Navigation Component?
sections.content: The Jetpack Navigation Component is a framework that simplifies implementing navigation between screens (destinations) in an Android app. It handles the complexity of Fragment transactions, back stack management, argument passing, deep linking, and transition animations — all from a single, visual navigation graph. It is the cornerstone of the Single Activity Architecture where one host Activity contains a NavHostFragment that swaps Fragments as the user navigates, replacing the old multi-Activity approach.

sections.heading: Why Use the Navigation Component?
items: Centralized navigation map — all destinations and paths are defined in a single navigation graph XML file
Automatic back stack management — handles Fragment transactions and back stack without manual FragmentManager calls
Type-safe argument passing — Safe Args plugin generates typed classes for passing data between destinations
Deep link support — handles both explicit and implicit deep links to any destination in the graph
Transition animations — define enter, exit, popEnter, and popExit animations on each action
NavigationUI integration — connects BottomNavigationView, DrawerLayout, and Toolbar to the nav graph automatically
ViewModel scoping — scope a ViewModel to a navigation subgraph for shared state across a subset of screens
Conditional navigation — navigate to different destinations based on state (e.g., login check)

rows.description: An XML resource file (res/navigation/) that defines all destinations and the actions (paths) between them
A screen or endpoint in the navigation graph — typically a Fragment, Activity, or dialog
A connection between two destinations defining how to navigate from one to the other
A special Fragment in the Activity layout that acts as the container — it swaps destinations in and out
The object that manages navigation within a NavHostFragment — call navigate() on it to trigger navigation
Represents a destination on the back stack — used for scoping ViewModels and retrieving results
A Gradle plugin that generates type-safe classes for passing arguments between destinations
A URI or pending intent that navigates directly to a specific destination in the graph

sections.heading: Setting Up the Navigation Component
sections.content: To use the Navigation Component, add the navigation-fragment and navigation-ui dependencies to your app-level build.gradle.kts. Optionally add the Safe Args Gradle plugin for type-safe argument passing. Then create a navigation graph resource file in res/navigation/, add a NavHostFragment to your Activity layout, and get a NavController reference to trigger navigation from your Fragments.

sections.heading: Setup Steps
items: Step 1: Add dependencies — implementation("androidx.navigation:navigation-fragment-ktx:2.7.x") and navigation-ui-ktx
Step 2: Add Safe Args plugin to build.gradle.kts plugins block: id("androidx.navigation.safeargs.kotlin")
Step 3: Create navigation graph — res/navigation/nav_graph.xml via New → Android Resource File → Navigation
Step 4: Add NavHostFragment to Activity layout with app:navGraph and app:defaultNavHost="true"
Step 5: Get NavController in a Fragment: val navController = findNavController()
Step 6: Navigate using actions: navController.navigate(R.id.action_home_to_detail) or Safe Args directions
Step 7: Connect NavigationUI for BottomNavigationView: NavigationUI.setupWithNavController(bottomNav, navController)
Step 8: Handle the Up button in Activity: navController.navigateUp() or NavigationUI.navigateUp(navController, appBarConfig)

rows.description: Root element of the graph; sets the app:startDestination to the first screen shown
Declares a Fragment destination with its android:id, android:name (class), and android:label
Declares an Activity as a destination — used when navigating to a separate Activity
Declares a DialogFragment as a destination — shown as an overlay without replacing the current screen
Defines a path from one destination to another with optional animations and popUpTo behavior
Declares a typed argument that must be passed when navigating to this destination
Associates a URI pattern with this destination for implicit deep link handling
Includes another navigation graph file — used for modular navigation in large apps

sections.heading: Navigating Between Destinations
items: Get NavController: findNavController() in a Fragment, or findNavController(R.id.nav_host_fragment) in Activity
Navigate by action ID: navController.navigate(R.id.action_homeFragment_to_detailFragment)
Navigate by destination ID: navController.navigate(R.id.detailFragment) — no action needed
Navigate with Safe Args directions: navController.navigate(HomeFragmentDirections.actionHomeToDetail(userId = 42))
Navigate with a Bundle: navController.navigate(R.id.detailFragment, bundleOf("userId" to 42))
Pop back stack: navController.popBackStack() — equivalent to pressing the Back button
Navigate and clear back stack: use popUpTo and popUpToInclusive in the action definition
Navigate to a deep link URI: navController.navigate(Uri.parse("myapp://detail/42"))

rows.description: The ID of the destination Fragment or screen this action navigates to
Pops the back stack up to (but not including) the specified destination before navigating
When true, also pops the popUpTo destination itself from the back stack
If true, avoids creating a duplicate of the destination if it is already at the top of the stack
Animation played when the destination enters the screen
Animation played when the current destination exits the screen
Animation played when the previous destination re-enters after popping the back stack
Animation played when the current destination exits when popped from the back stack

sections.heading: Safe Args — Type-Safe Navigation
sections.content: Safe Args is a Gradle plugin that processes your navigation graph and generates type-safe Kotlin classes for every destination that has arguments and every action that navigates between them. Instead of building a Bundle manually and risking typos or wrong types, you use the generated Directions class to create navigation calls and the generated Args class to retrieve arguments in the destination Fragment. Safe Args catches argument type mismatches at compile time rather than at runtime.

sections.heading: Safe Args — Passing & Receiving Arguments
items: Declare arguments in the navigation graph: <argument android:name="userId" app:argType="integer" android:defaultValue="0" />
Supported argument types: integer, float, long, boolean, string, resource reference, custom Parcelable, Serializable, Enum
Navigate with generated Directions: navController.navigate(HomeFragmentDirections.actionHomeToDetail(userId = 42))
Retrieve in destination Fragment using the generated Args class: val args: DetailFragmentArgs by navArgs()
Access the argument: val userId = args.userId
by navArgs() is a Kotlin property delegate that lazily reads arguments from the Fragment's argument Bundle
Default values in <argument> make the argument optional — navigation still works without explicitly passing it
For nullable String: app:argType="string" with app:nullable="true" and android:defaultValue="@null"

sections.heading: Safe Args vs Manual Bundle Comparison

rows.safe_args: Yes — argument types enforced at compile time
rows.manual_bundle: No — wrong type causes runtime crash

rows.safe_args: Yes — nullable arguments declared explicitly in graph
Safe — renaming argument updates generated class; compile error if mismatched
rows.manual_bundle: Risky — string keys can silently mismatch

rows.safe_args: High — IDE autocompletes generated Directions and Args classes
rows.manual_bundle: Low — must remember key strings manually
Use only for non-Navigation Component cases

sections.heading: NavigationUI — Connecting to App Bars and Bottom Navigation
sections.content: NavigationUI is a utility class that connects your navigation graph to common UI patterns like the top app bar, bottom navigation bar, and navigation drawer. It automatically updates the toolbar title to match the destination label, handles the Up button, and highlights the correct tab in the BottomNavigationView based on the current destination. Using NavigationUI eliminates the manual wiring of back stack and title management.

sections.heading: NavigationUI Setup Patterns
items: Get NavController in Activity: val navController = findNavController(R.id.nav_host_fragment)
Set up AppBar: NavigationUI.setupActionBarWithNavController(this, navController, appBarConfiguration)
Handle Up button in Activity: override onSupportNavigateUp() { return navController.navigateUp(appBarConfiguration) || super.onSupportNavigateUp() }
Set up BottomNavigationView: NavigationUI.setupWithNavController(bottomNavigationView, navController)
Set up NavigationView (drawer): NavigationUI.setupWithNavController(navigationView, navController)
Define top-level destinations in AppBarConfiguration: AppBarConfiguration(setOf(R.id.homeFragment, R.id.profileFragment))
Top-level destinations show a hamburger icon instead of the Back arrow in the toolbar
NavigationUI.setupWithNavController on BottomNavigationView handles tab switching and back stack automatically

rows.scenario: Navigate to Home and clear entire back stack (e.g., after login)
rows.setup: app:popUpTo="@id/loginFragment" app:popUpToInclusive="true" on the action to homeFragment

rows.scenario: Bottom nav tab switching without stacking destinations
rows.setup: NavigationUI.setupWithNavController handles this automatically with saveState and restoreState

rows.scenario: Navigate to a destination and avoid duplicates
Go back to a specific destination in the back stack
rows.setup: navController.popBackStack(R.id.homeFragment, false) — false means don't pop homeFragment itself

rows.scenario: Clear back stack to root and relaunch

sections.heading: Passing Results Between Destinations
items: Use the Fragment Result API for passing one-time results back from a destination to the previous one
In the destination sending the result: setFragmentResult("requestKey", bundleOf("result" to value))
In the previous destination: setFragmentResultListener("requestKey") { _, bundle -> val result = bundle.getString("result") }
For ViewModel-scoped results, use a shared ViewModel scoped to the nav graph with by navGraphViewModels(R.id.nav_graph)
navController.previousBackStackEntry?.savedStateHandle?.set("key", value) — alternative for passing results via back stack
navController.currentBackStackEntry?.savedStateHandle?.getLiveData<Type>("key") — observe result in the previous destination
The SavedStateHandle approach is simpler than the Fragment Result API for Navigation Component use cases
Avoid passing large objects as results — pass IDs and let the receiver fetch the full object from the ViewModel

rows.description: Created programmatically using NavDeepLinkBuilder — used for notifications and widgets that navigate to a specific destination
Triggered by a URI, registered in the nav graph with <deepLink> and in AndroidManifest with <nav-graph>
rows.example: <deepLink app:uri="myapp://detail/{userId}" /> in nav graph; <nav-graph android:value="@navigation/nav_graph" /> in manifest

rows.description: Handles https:// URLs — requires App Links verification with a Digital Asset Links JSON file on your server

sections.heading: Scoping ViewModel to a Navigation Graph
items: Use by navGraphViewModels(R.id.nav_graph) in a Fragment to scope a ViewModel to the entire navigation graph
All Fragments within that nav graph share the same ViewModel instance
The ViewModel is cleared when the user navigates out of the nav graph entirely
Ideal for multi-step form flows where data accumulates across multiple screens
For a subgraph: nest destinations in a <navigation> element with its own ID and use that ID in navGraphViewModels
This is more precise than activityViewModels() — the ViewModel is cleared when the subgraph is exited
Use it for checkout flows, onboarding sequences, or any wizard-style multi-screen interaction
The NavBackStackEntry for the graph is the LifecycleOwner for the graph-scoped ViewModel

sections.heading: Common Navigation Component Mistakes to Avoid

rows.fix: Check currentDestination before navigating: if (navController.currentDestination?.id == R.id.homeFragment) navigate()
Add app:defaultNavHost="true" so NavHostFragment intercepts the system Back button

rows.mistake: Using FragmentManager directly alongside Navigation Component
rows.fix: Let the Navigation Component manage all Fragment transactions — mixing causes back stack conflicts
Implement @Parcelize on data classes or pass IDs and fetch the full object in the destination ViewModel

rows.mistake: Forgetting to add <nav-graph> to AndroidManifest for deep links
Observing NavController destination changes in Fragment instead of Activity
rows.fix: Add OnDestinationChangedListener to NavController in the Activity for global destination tracking

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: The Jetpack Navigation Component transforms screen navigation from a fragile, manually managed collection of Fragment transactions into a declarative, type-safe, visually inspectable system. By centralizing all destinations and paths in a navigation graph, using Safe Args for argument passing, NavigationUI for toolbar and tab integration, and NavGraph-scoped ViewModels for shared state, you get a navigation architecture that is robust, maintainable, and perfectly aligned with the Single Activity Architecture that modern Android development recommends.
